name: Toyota Grand Highlander Hybrid XLE
why: Scores the maximum 5 on four qualitative criteria simultaneously: car seat fit, 3rd row comfort, corridor performance, and reliability. No other candidate achieves this combination. The 3rd row offers 39.5" of legroom — genuinely adult-comfortable on a 2-hour corridor drive — while the standard 8-seat bench accommodates three car seats comfortably. Toyota's #1 Consumer Reports reliability rating and the 10-year/240,000 km hybrid battery warranty make this the lowest-risk 10-year ownership proposition. Its TCO score of 3.10 reflects a genuine advantage over the used version: the new vehicle's projected residual at year 10 (~$27,000) significantly exceeds the used version's (~$13,000).
tradeoffs: At ~$62,700 on-road it is the most expensive new purchase in this comparison. Fuel costs of ~$16,900 over 10 years are substantially higher than PHEV and BEV alternatives (~$9,000–11,500 more). No plug-in capability. Whether the year-10 residual projections are reliable enough to justify buying new over the used version (rank 2) is a legitimate question — the ~$10,000 purchase price gap is real cash today; the residual difference is a projection.

name: Toyota Sienna Hybrid XLE AWD

specs.third_row_legroom: Comfortable for kids; short trips for adults

why: Same Sienna Hybrid platform as the used candidate at rank 3, with two material upgrades for a 10-year hold: full 10-year/240,000 km hybrid battery warranty starts fresh from purchase (used 2022–23 examples have 6–7 yrs remaining), and the year-10 residual projection is materially higher (~$23k vs ~$11k for the used). The premium over the used version is ~$7k on-road; whether that's worth it depends on how much weight you put on the warranty tail and the residual delta.
tradeoffs: Highest on-road price of any minivan in the comparison. Ground clearance of 170 mm is among the lowest in the field. The minivan format limits FSR utility (sliding doors and low body are highway/urban features). Same fuel economy as the used Sienna Hybrid; no electrification beyond the hybrid system.

name: Toyota Grand Highlander Hybrid XLE
why: Qualitative scores are identical to the new version across every criterion — same vehicle, same capabilities. The gap from rank 1 is entirely due to a lower TCO score (2.05 vs 3.10), reflecting a higher net TCO driven by a lower projected year-10 residual on a used vehicle. At ~$52,400 on-road versus ~$62,700 new, the lower purchase price is real and substantial.
tradeoffs: Same qualitative trade-offs as rank 1: high fuel cost vs PHEV/BEV, no plug-in, towing requires Hybrid MAX trim (verify at purchase). Hybrid battery warranty transfer should be verified at point of sale. A 2023–24 example will be 11–13 years old at the end of the ownership horizon. This is the better choice if you are sceptical of 10-year residual projections or prefer to avoid the premium of buying new.
caution: Verify Toyota 10yr/240,000km hybrid battery warranty transfer at purchase.

name: Chrysler Pacifica Hybrid Touring L
why: The only PHEV minivan available in the comparison — 51 km of EV range covers most Metro Vancouver daily driving on home electricity, and the 10-year/240,000 km Stellantis traction-battery warranty has ~7 yrs left at purchase. Sliding doors + flat floor + genuine adult-friendly 3rd row are the minivan structural advantages. 8-year/160k km PHEV component warranty is U.S. only — in Canada the Stellantis warranty is 8yr/130k on hybrid system + 10yr/240k on battery.
tradeoffs: Stellantis reliability is a real concern — Consumer Reports rates the Pacifica below average, with electrical-system complaints concentrated in the hybrid version. Cargo is excellent but ground clearance of 130 mm is the lowest in this field — FSR use is not on the table. FWD only (no AWD on Pacifica Hybrid). Used supply in BC is thin.
caution: Pacifica Hybrid Canadian warranty is 8yr/130k on hybrid drive + 10yr/240k on traction battery. Verify both at purchase, plus any open recalls (early 22 hybrids had a software stop-sale that's since been resolved).

name: Toyota Sienna Hybrid Woodland AWD

specs.third_row_legroom: Comfortable for kids; short trips for adults

why: Scores the maximum 5 on car seat fit, cargo utility, and corridor performance. The sliding rear doors are a daily convenience with young children that no SUV in this comparison matches. Cargo at 949 L behind the third row is nearly double the Sorento PHEV. Fuel economy at ~6.5 L/100km is the best non-plug-in vehicle here, producing a ~1,077 km tank range. Toyota #1 reliability and 10-year/240,000 km hybrid battery warranty.
tradeoffs: Ground clearance of 160–175 mm is the lowest of all 12 candidates — a highway and urban vehicle; FSR use is not recommended. The minivan format is a personal preference — functionally it is the most practical layout for a family of five with young children.
caution: Verify Toyota 10yr/240,000km hybrid battery warranty transfer at purchase.

name: Honda Odyssey EX-L / Touring
why: The ICE minivan benchmark. Magic Slide 2nd row genuinely solves three-car-seat geometry better than any SUV in this comparison — the centre seat can offset or remove without disturbing the outboards, making a clear walkthrough to the 3rd row. Adult-friendly 3rd row, 929 L cargo behind it, Honda's strong reliability record on this generation, and the largest used supply of any minivan candidate in BC.
tradeoffs: FWD only — no AWD ever offered on the Odyssey. Fuel costs of ~$22k over 10 years are typical for a V6 minivan. Ground clearance of 115 mm is the lowest in the entire field — strictly a highway/urban vehicle. No hybrid or electrified Odyssey in this generation. Factory powertrain warranty expires by 2027 on most used examples.

name: Subaru Ascent Touring AWD

specs.third_row_legroom: Tight for adults; fine for children

why: Scores the maximum 5 on corridor performance, hitch compatibility, reliability, and FSR capability. The FSR score of 5 is unique — 221 mm ground clearance with Subaru's Symmetrical full-time AWD and X-Mode terrain management is the most capable system in this comparison. Subaru ranks #2 at Consumer Reports. Lowest purchase price in the comparison (~$38,500 on-road).
tradeoffs: Fuel costs of ~$23,000 over 10 years are among the highest — ICE-only with no electrification path. The 3rd row is tight for adults. Factory warranty exhausted on 2022–23 examples.

name: Kia Carnival SX / SX+
why: Kia's reinterpretation of the minivan as a unibody MPV — slightly higher ride height than the Odyssey/Pacifica (170 mm vs 115–130) and the most cargo capacity of any vehicle in this comparison at 1,135 L behind the third row. Adult-friendly 3rd row, sliding doors, 8-seat configuration on SX trim. Lowest purchase price of any minivan candidate.
tradeoffs: FWD only — same AWD-absent constraint as Odyssey and Pacifica. 2022–23 reliability is mid-pack (Kia brand has improved but the Carnival's first-generation transmission and electronics have an unsettled record). 5yr/100k powertrain warranty expires by 2027 on most used examples. Factory hitch is a dealer-install option on SX trim — verify or budget aftermarket.
caution: First-generation Carnival (2022+); some early production had electrical-system complaints. Verify recall history at purchase.

name: Toyota Highlander Hybrid XLE AWD
why: Toyota #1 reliability and the 10-year/240,000 km hybrid battery warranty at the lowest purchase price of any Toyota hybrid in this comparison. The best TCO score of the three Toyota hybrid candidates at 2.92. Note: this is the standard Highlander, NOT the Grand Highlander — a smaller vehicle.
tradeoffs: The 3rd row scores a 2 — approximately 30" of legroom, comfortable for children but not suitable for adults on a 2-hour corridor run. This is why it ranks below both Grand Highlander variants despite better TCO. Towing is 3,500 lbs. Verify 8-seat bench availability on XLE trim at purchase.
caution: Standard Highlander — NOT the Grand Highlander. Smaller interior and tighter 3rd row. Verify 8-seat bench and hybrid battery warranty transfer at purchase.

name: Hyundai Palisade Preferred/Urban AWD
why: The most affordable way to get a genuinely adult-friendly 3rd row in this comparison at ~$40,700 on-road. Interior quality is widely regarded as the most refined of any non-luxury 3-row SUV at this price. AWD is standard on all Canadian trims. Strong Vancouver supply with 45+ listings within 50 km.
tradeoffs: Fuel costs of ~$24,000 over 10 years are the joint highest. Reliability rated 'average' at Consumer Reports. A wiper motor recall affects 2021–2024 models — verify completion at purchase. No hybrid option in this generation.
caution: Wiper motor recall affects 2021–2024 models (failure in ice/snow buildup). Verify recall completion before purchase.

name: Kia Telluride EX AWD
why: The segment benchmark. Scores 5 on car seat fit, corridor performance, and hitch compatibility, and a 4 on 3rd row comfort. Consumer Reports rates the Telluride above the Kia brand average. 5,500 lb tow rating leads the field. Build quality is a genuine benchmark for the segment.
tradeoffs: TCO score of 1.00 — the worst in the field — reflects $100,920 in net 10-year TCO, driven almost entirely by ~$24,000 in fuel costs. The Telluride is the right choice if 3rd row adult comfort and driving experience are weighted heavily enough to justify the running cost premium.

name: Tesla Model Y Long Range AWD

specs.third_row_legroom: No 3rd row (2023+ Model Y is 5-seat only in Canada)

why: Same constraints and benefits as the new Model Y at a much lower entry price (~$50k on-road vs ~$73k). Traction-battery warranty has ~6 years remaining on 2023–24 examples (8yr/192k from in-service date). Strong used supply in BC compared to other BEVs.
tradeoffs: Same five-seat constraint as the new version. Used Tesla resale weakened materially through 2024–25 — projected year-10 residual is one of the lowest in the field as a percentage of purchase price. Battery health (and any warranty service history) varies widely on used examples; insist on the Service Mode battery health report at purchase.
caution: Five-seat only. Confirm remaining traction-battery warranty and request a Service Mode battery-degradation report from the dealer before purchase.

name: Honda Pilot EX-L/Touring AWD
why: Unique removable/stowable second-row centre seat: 8-seat bench with latch removal in under 30 seconds, creating a clear walkthrough lane to the 3rd row without disturbing car seats. Honda CPO certification (7yr/160K warranty) may be available. Honda top-tier reliability. Ground clearance of 220 mm is second-highest in the comparison.
tradeoffs: ICE only — no hybrid Pilot in Canada. Fuel costs of ~$22,000 over 10 years. 3rd row is tight for adults on longer trips. This vehicle is likely undervalued by the matrix — the removable centre seat is a practical daily feature the scoring doesn't fully capture.
caution: 2023 or newer ONLY. The 2022 Pilot is a prior-generation vehicle — do not confuse them.

name: Kia Sorento PHEV EX
why: The lowest 10-year net TCO of any candidate ($77,440) and a perfect 5.00 TCO score. A 57 km electric range covers most Vancouver daily driving on home electricity, while 710 km combined range eliminates corridor anxiety. Fuel and energy costs of ~$7,800 over 10 years are dramatically lower than any ICE candidate.
tradeoffs: 3rd row is children-only — a structural limitation no trim resolves. Cargo at 357 L behind the third row is the smallest of all candidates. Tow rating 1,654 lbs. If 3rd row is genuinely children-only for you, this vehicle's low TCO may warrant reconsideration despite ranking 9th.
caution: Verify PHEV battery warranty transferability at purchase. 3rd row is children-only — not suitable for adult passengers.

name: Mazda CX-90 PHEV GS
why: TCO score of 3.77 (second best in the field). Good qualitative scores on car seat fit, winter capability, and FSR. 8-seat bench standard on all trims. Premium interior quality. 203 mm clearance with i-ACTIV AWD.
tradeoffs: Reliability score of 1 — Consumer Reports rates 'well below average' in its second model year. This is the primary risk for a 10-year ownership horizon. 3.3 kW onboard charger takes ~6 hours for full charge. Thin BC used supply.
caution: Consumer Reports 'well-below average' reliability in year 2. Significant risk for 10-year ownership. Verify carefully at purchase.

name: Kia EV9 Land AWD
why: Scores maximum 5 on hitch compatibility. 800V/350 kW charging recovers ~1,000 km of range per hour. Lowest energy costs of any candidate at ~$5,400 over 10 years. Kia won Canadian Black Book Best Retained Value for BEV SUVs.
tradeoffs: Car seat fit scores a 3 (bench tight with three car seats). On-road price of ~$62,100 is the highest used candidate. Unknown battery history — verify warranty status. By 2034 will be a 10-year-old first-generation EV. Requires one planned charging stop Vancouver–Kamloops.
caution: Used BEV: verify battery health history and remaining warranty coverage before purchase.

name: Tesla Model Y Long Range AWD

specs.third_row_legroom: No 3rd row (2023+ Model Y is 5-seat only in Canada)

why: Included for benchmarking, not as a family-fit recommendation: best-in-class fast-charging via Supercharger, ~17 kWh/100km efficiency yields the lowest fuel cost of any candidate, and Tesla's drivetrain warranty (8yr/192k km traction battery) covers the cliff years of this hold. If two-row seating with cargo is sufficient, this is the easiest BEV ownership in the comparison.
tradeoffs: Five seats only — 2023+ Canadian Model Ys do not offer the optional 3rd row, so three car seats across the back row is a structural daily constraint with no overflow capacity for guests. Reliability scores 3 (Consumer Reports rates 'average' with persistent panel-gap and trim complaints on this generation). No hitch from the factory in Canada — accessory hitch (1.25" or 2") is dealer-fit. Highest insurance premium of any candidate.
caution: Five-seat only. If a 6th or 7th passenger is ever required, this vehicle does not fit the family.

name: Hyundai IONIQ 9 Preferred AWD
why: Best BEV 3rd row in the comparison (score 4), widest interior, 539 km of range, 800V/350 kW charging. May be the most compelling BEV to revisit in 2027 once reliability data accumulates and used examples appear.
tradeoffs: First-year model with no reliability history — score 2. TCO score of 2.01 near top of range due to $75,100 purchase price. Ground clearance of 177 mm is the lowest of all 12 candidates. Not the right choice to buy new today, but worth watching for a 2027 used purchase.
caution: First-year model (2026) — no reliability history. Consider waiting for 2027 used examples with established track record.
